top_insights: The power of storytelling and narrative in business and leadership
Financial leverage and tangible income signals (e.g., PayPal hits, product pricing)
Personal discipline habits: journaling, routines, and self-discipline
Creator-led entrepreneurship and solving real-world problems
Contrarian takes on industry hype (e.g., AI, competition, business advice)
hook_patterns: Direct financial or status claims (e.g., '$5,000 hit', 'best business advice')
Contrarian statements challenging popular opinion or hype
Authoritative 'single most powerful' or 'all you need' declarations
emotional_drivers: Validation (feeling confirmed in beliefs or status)
Ambition (desire for growth, success, and leverage)
Security (seeking clarity, calm, and control over one's life)
emerging_narrative: Audiences are resonating with authentic, high-leverage success stories that blend personal discipline with tangible financial results. There is a strong shift away from abstract theory toward 'builder' narratives—where creators solve specific problems, build real businesses, and validate their success through concrete metrics rather than just hype.

viral_examples.author: dickiebush
viral_examples.text: “Cha-ching” $5,000 hit my PayPal account. “This has to be a typo...” I thought to myself. So I messaged him. “Hey, did you mean to send $5,000?” His response: “Yeah, is that enough?” And just like that, Ghostwriting completely changed my life trajectory. Here’s the story

viral_examples.author: dickiebush
viral_examples.text: Steve Jobs once said: "The most powerful person is the storyteller." And luckily, storytelling is a skill. But most people have never tried to learn – because no one showed them where to start. So here are 6 of the best storytelling threads (so you can start learning today):

viral_examples.author: dickiebush
viral_examples.text: The single most powerful habit for personal growth: Journaling. Over the past 5 years, I've journaled every single morning—and along the way, I've: •Answered over 1,000 questions •Tested every app, pen, & notebook But I always return to a pen, paper, and these 5 prompts:

viral_examples.author: dickiebush
viral_examples.text: The most creative company of the last 30 years: Pixar. Back in 2011, Pixar storyboard artist Emma Coats shared their "22 Rules For Storytelling." And the rules are a must-read for writers, entrepreneurs, and anyone who wants to tell captivating stories. Here's the breakdown:

viral_examples.author: dickiebush
viral_examples.text: ChatGPT is overhyped. That's what I told myself after 2 weeks of trying (and failing) to use it well. Turns out, I was just a poor prompt writer. But after spending hundreds of hours tinkering, I've finally cracked it. And now, it's my personal writing assistant. Here's how:

winning_patterns: Direct financial or status claims (e.g., '$5,000 hit', 'best business advice')
Contrarian statements challenging popular opinion or hype
Authoritative 'single most powerful' or 'all you need' declarations
